note: This is a conference presentation by the CEO of Animaker with embedded video clips featuring a quoted AI researcher, a customer service role-play scenario, a training avatar character, and dubbed speech samples.

speakers.name: Animaker CEO

speakers.name: Dario Amodei (quoted)

speakers.full_transcript: Just before OpenAI started, I met Ilya. One of the first things he said to me was, "Look, the models, they just wanna learn. You have to understand this. The models, they just wanna learn." It was a bit like a Zen koan. Like, I listened to this and, and I became enlightened. The models just wanna learn. You get the obstacles out of their way, right? You give them good data. You give them enough space to operate in. You don't do something stupid like condition them badly numerically, um, and they, they wanna learn. They'll do it. They'll do it. Great. He wasn't talking about student training, he wasn't talking about employee training or plane training, right? He was talking about AI itself.

speakers.name: Training Avatar (AI character)
speakers.role: Avatar instructor in course design framework video
speakers.context: Appears in embedded video from S156-S199 introducing herself as a trainer and providing a framework for choosing course formats. Distinct voice and perspective from the main presenter, speaking directly to learners about course design.
speakers.full_transcript: Hey, I am a trainer just like you. And honestly, one of the biggest questions I kept asking myself when I started designing courses was, what format should I even use? Should I do a PowerPoint, a video, role play, games, quizzes, microlearning? The options felt overwhelming and picking the wrong one, that can kill engagement completely. So today, I am going to give you a simple framework to pick the right course style every single time. Here's the first mistake most trainers make, including me. We pick the format we enjoy creating, not the format our learners actually need. So before you choose anything, ask yourself three questions. Who is your learner? What do they need to do after this training? And where will they consume it? Everything flows from those three answers. Let me quickly walk you through when to use each format. PowerPoint or slides. Use this for concept-heavy content like policies or data. Keep it structured and add interaction. Video. Use video for demos or storytelling. Keep it short, clear, and focused. Role play. Use role play when learners need to practice real-world skills. Microlearning. Great for busy learners. Keep it short and focused on one idea. Interactive games. Use games to boost engagement, motivation, and repeated practice. Quizzes. Use quizzes to reinforce learning at the start or end of a training module. Okay, so here's the simple framework I use every time I design a course. Ask yourself three questions. Question one. Is this about knowledge or skill? For knowledge, use slides, video, or microlearning. For skill, use role play, games, or simulations. Question two. How motivated is your learner? If motivation is high, most formats work. If it's low, use games or interactive content. Question three. How much time do they have? If they have time, use videos or role play. If time is limited, use microlearning or quick quizzes. Run any topic through these questions and the right format becomes clear. Here's the truth. There is no single perfect format. The best trainers mix and match. Start with your learner's need. Match the format to the outcome. And don't be afraid to blend. A short video followed by a quiz followed by a role play? That's a powerful learning journey. You don't need to pick one style. All right.

sentences.text: Hey, I am a trainer just like you.

And honestly, one of the biggest questions I kept asking myself when I started designing courses was, what format should I even use? So today, I am going to give you a simple framework to pick the right course style every single time.
Here's the first mistake most trainers make, including me.
We pick the format we enjoy creating, not the format our learners actually need.
So before you choose anything, ask yourself three questions.
Who is your learner? What do they need to do after this training? And where will they consume it? Everything flows from those three answers.
Let me quickly walk you through when to use each format.
PowerPoint or slides.
Use this for concept-heavy content like policies or data.
Keep it structured and add interaction.
Video.

Keep it short, clear, and focused.
Role play.
Use role play when learners need to practice real-world skills.
Microlearning.
Great for busy learners.
Keep it short and focused on one idea.
Interactive games.
Use games to boost engagement, motivation, and repeated practice.
Quizzes.
Use quizzes to reinforce learning at the start or end of a training module.
Okay, so here's the simple framework I use every time I design a course.
Ask yourself three questions.
Question one.
Is this about knowledge or skill? For knowledge, use slides, video, or microlearning.
For skill, use role play, games, or simulations.
Question two.
How motivated is your learner? If motivation is high, most formats work.
If it's low, use games or interactive content.
Question three.
How much time do they have? If they have time, use videos or role play.
If time is limited, use microlearning or quick quizzes.
Run any topic through these questions and the right format becomes clear.
Here's the truth.
There is no single perfect format.
The best trainers mix and match.
Start with your learner's need.
Match the format to the outcome.
And don't be afraid to blend.
A short video followed by a quiz followed by a role play? That's a powerful learning journey.
You don't need to pick one style.
